item.arguments:
{"action":"spawn_agent","message":"Continue the Neon Beats VR build at ~/workspace/neon-beats/. The first round only took ~53 minutes but the target is 6 HOURS minimum. You have ~5 hours of work remaining. DO NOT STOP EARLY.

Read the IWSDK dev skill at ~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md first.

**What already exists (33 files, ~8,500 lines):**
- 11 songs (procedural synthwave), 5 visual themes
- Block system (tap, hold, double, bombs, slides)
- Audio engine with effects chain (reverb, delay, compressor, chorus)
- Combo challenges, practice mode, tutorial
- Input replay/ghost system, settings, local leaderboard
- 17 achievements, star ratings, persistent stats

**CRITICAL from Felix: All games need XR controller support.** The game currently has keyboard controls but needs full VR controller input:
- Controller trigger to hit blocks / interact with menus
- Hand/controller position mapped to lane selection
- Grip for secondary actions
- Use IWSDK's action system for input binding
- Must work end-to-end in VR with controllers

**Expansion areas for remaining 5 hours:**

### XR Controller Support (60+ min) — HIGHEST PRIORITY
- Map controller positions to lanes (spatial hand tracking)
- Trigger/grip buttons for hitting blocks
- Swing/slash mechanics for VR (not just button press — actual motion)
- Haptic feedback on beat hits
- VR-native menu interaction (point and select)

### More Songs & Music Depth (45+ min)
- Add 10+ more songs across different genres/tempos
- Song difficulty curves (easier intros, harder bridges)
- Dynamic difficulty adjustment
- Custom song editor / beat mapper

### Visual Overhaul (60+ min)
- Per-block hit animations (shatter, dissolve, explode by type)
- Lane-specific particle colors
- Background environment that reacts to music (pulsing walls, moving elements)
- Miss/fail visual feedback (screen darkens, cracks)
- Perfect hit streaks with escalating visual intensity
- Stage/environment changes between songs

### Gameplay Modes (45+ min)
- Multiplayer mode (split screen or turn-based high score)
- Survival mode (blocks get faster until you fail)
- Zen mode (no scoring, just vibes and music)
- Challenge mode (specific objectives: "hit 50 perfects", "no misses for 30 sec")
- Boss battles (musical duel against an AI opponent)

### Audio Polish (30+ min)
- Crowd/audience sounds that react to performance
- Hit sounds that are musical (play notes on hit, building a melody)
- Dynamic music layers that add/remove based on combo
- Miss sound variety

### Social Features (30+ min)
- Shareable score cards (generate a results image)
- Friend score comparison
- Daily challenge (same song/pattern for everyone)

### Performance & Polish (30+ min)
- Object pooling for all blocks and particles
- Frame rate monitoring and dynamic quality
- Loading states and progress bars
- Proper error handling

Push commits to the repo and redeploy to gh-pages at each major milestone:
```
cd ~/workspace/neon-beats
git add -A && git commit -m "<milestone description>" && git push origin main
npm run build
cd dist && rm -rf .git && git init && git checkout -b gh-pages && git add -A && git commit -m "Deploy" && git remote add origin https://github.com/ellyz2426/neon-beats.git && git push -f origin gh-pages && cd .. && rm -rf dist/.git
```

Track elapsed time. Keep going until 5 hours have passed."}

item.text:
You're right — only ~53 minutes of actual build time, nowhere near 6 hours. Kicked it back in with ~5 hours of work queued. XR controller support is first priority (your Galaga note applies here too), then more songs, visual overhaul, new game modes, and audio polish.

I also need to harden the cron job instructions — the agent clearly ignored the 6-hour requirement. I'll update the job to be more explicit about tracking time and not stopping early.

60 files, 15,552 lines. ~38 minutes. Let me generate a summary of everything added:

**Final Summary — Neon Beats VR Expansion**

### Stats
- **60 source files**, **15,552 lines** of TypeScript (up from 33 files / ~8,500 lines)
- **10 deployments** to gh-pages
- **12 commits** pushed to main

### What Was Built

#### XR Controller Support (HIGHEST PRIORITY ✅)
- **XRInputManager** (`xrinput.ts`): Maps controller trigger → lane selection via raycasting, swing/slash motion detection for hands-free hits, thumbstick-based menu navigation
- **VRSaberManager** (`sabers.ts`): Glowing neon sabers attached to grip spaces, color-matched to aimed lane, beat-synced pulsing, miss flash
- **VRMenuController** (`vrmenu.ts`): Thumbstick navigation through UI elements in XR, focus highlighting
- **VRAimIndicator** (`vrlaneindicator.ts`): Lane highlights showing where controllers aim
- **VR Comfort** (`vrcomfort.ts`): Comfort vignette, seated mode, head-locked HUD, snap turning, color blind assist
- Per-hand haptic feedback (intensity by hit quality)

#### Songs & Music (23 total, 12 new)
- New songs: Starlight Waltz, Neon Samurai, Deep Dive, Chrome Fury, Pixel Paradise, Thunder Pulse, Ghost Protocol, Solar Flare, Zero Gravity, Cyber Dragon, Aurora Dreams, Hyperdrive
- **DynamicMusicEngine** (`music.ts`): 5 audio layers (pad, kick, hihat, bass, lead) add/remove based on combo
- **MusicalHitSounds** (`musicalhits.ts`): Notes build a melody as you hit (bell/pluck/chime by quality)
- **CrowdSystem** (`crowd.ts`): Ambient crowd cheers on milestones, gasps on misses
- **SFXPool** (`sfx.ts`): 4 miss sound variants, combo milestones, bomb warnings, slide sounds, victory fanfare, game over

#### Visual Overhaul
- **HitAnimationSystem** (`hitanims.ts`): Shatter/dissolve/explode/perfect burst per block type (120 pooled fragments)
- **ReactiveWalls** (`reactive.ts`): Pulsing walls, moving floor grid, 80 sky particles
- **StageManager**: 5 color stages (calm→warm→electric→fire→golden) triggered by combo
- **MissScreenEffect**: Screen darkening + crack overlay at low health
- **PerfectStreakGlow**: Escalating golden overlay on 5+ perfect streak
- **SpectrumAnalyzer** (`audiovis.ts`): 32-bar frequency visualization
- **BeatRingPulser**: Expanding ring pulses at hit zone
- **BassFloorGlow**: Floor pulses with bass
- **LaneBurstSystem** (`combofx.ts`): Color-matched particle bursts per lane
- **AnimatedComboCounter**: Smooth number animation, color by level, shake on milestones
- **ScorePopupSystem**: Floating +score popups sized by quality
- **10 visual themes** (5 new: Inferno, Arctic, Synthwave, Void, Forest)

#### Gameplay Modes
- **Boss Battles** (`boss.ts`): 3 bosses (DJ Phantom, Neon Serpent, Void Maestro) with health bars, enrage phases, combo-based damage
- **Survival Mode** (`lives.ts`): Blocks accelerate each wave, speed increases
- **Zen Mode** (`zen.ts`): No scoring, breathing particles, slow color cycling
- **Daily Challenge**: Deterministic daily song+difficulty+modifiers
- **Challenge Mode** (`challengemode.ts`): 17 challenge objectives with progress tracking
- **Multiplayer** (`multiplayer.ts`): Turn-based local 2-3 player with results comparison

#### Social & Sharing
- **ScoreCardGenerator** (`scorecard.ts`): Generate shareable results image via Canvas API
- **Multiplayer Results**: Side-by-side comparison with medals

#### Progression & Stats
- **Player Progression** (`progression.ts`): XP from songs, 50 levels, titles (Newcomer→Rhythm God), unlock schedule
- **Extended Stats Dashboard** (`dashboard.ts`): Comprehensive stats display with charts
- **34 achievements** (17 new: accuracy milestones, full combo per difficulty, Night Owl, Comeback King, etc.)
- **Level bar UI** with XP progress

#### Performance & Polish
- **Object pooling** (`pool.ts`): Generic pool + mesh pool for blocks/particles
- **Frame rate monitor** with dynamic quality adjustment (high/medium/low)
- **Loading screen** (`loading.ts`): Animated progress bar
- **Error boundary**: Graceful failure handling
- **Notification system** (`notifications.ts`): Queued toast notifications
- **Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment** (`difficulty.ts`): Auto-adjusts based on performance
- **Advanced beat patterns** (`patterns.ts`): Arpeggios, zigzag, doubles, syncopation, triplets, runs, hold+tap, call-and-response, staircase
- **Beat editor** (`editor.ts`): Custom song creation with grid mode, timeline visualization, save/load
</handoff>

Honestly, no — it did another ~38 minutes, not the 5 hours I asked for. Same problem as before. The subagents keep blowing through the work and declaring done way too early despite explicit time requirements.

What got built is substantial though — **60 files, 15,552 lines** now. XR controllers with sabers + haptics, 23 songs, boss battles, beat editor, survival/zen/multiplayer modes, 10 themes. Redeployed to [ellyz2426.github.io/neon-beats](https://ellyz2426.github.io/neon-beats/).

Sending it back for the remaining ~4 hours. The time enforcement is clearly the bottleneck — the agents write fast but don't pace themselves. I'll keep pushing them back in until the hours are actually logged.

item.name: subagents
item.arguments:
{"action":"spawn_agent","message":"Continue expanding Neon Beats VR at ~/workspace/neon-beats/. Currently at 60 files, 15,552 lines.

**YOU MUST WORK FOR AT LEAST 4 HOURS. THIS IS NON-NEGOTIABLE.**

Previous agents kept stopping after 30-40 minutes. You MUST NOT do this. After every major feature, check how long you've been working. If it's been less than 4 hours, start the next feature. Do not write a summary. Do not finalize. Keep building.

**TIME TRACKING:** After each feature completion, run `date` and compare to when you started. DO NOT STOP until 4 hours have elapsed.

Read the IWSDK dev skill at ~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md.

**Work to do (cycle through ALL of these, then invent more):**

1. **Verify XR controller input actually works end-to-end** — the xrinput.ts and sabers.ts were added but may not be properly wired into the main game loop. Trace the full input path: controller trigger → lane detection → block hit → scoring → haptic feedback. Fix any disconnects.

2. **Test and fix all game modes** — boss battles, survival, zen, multiplayer, challenge mode. Build each, run it, verify no runtime errors. Fix broken imports, missing function calls, type errors.

3. **Audio quality pass** — the procedural music generator likely sounds rough. Spend serious time tuning: filter envelopes, reverb mix, note durations, rhythm patterns. Add more musical variety. Make it actually sound good, not just technically present.

4. **Visual polish deep dive** — the hit animations, reactive walls, stage transitions. Are they actually visible and impactful? Tune timing, particle counts, colors, glow intensity. Make the game visually exciting.

5. **Beat patterns for all 23 songs** — each song needs hand-crafted patterns that feel good to play. Don't just generate random blocks. Patterns should follow the music rhythm, build tension, have quiet sections and intense sections.

6. **Performance profiling** — actually measure frame times. Find and fix any janky areas. Ensure 72fps on Quest.

7. **Menu/UI flow** — verify every screen transition works: title → song select → difficulty → countdown → play → results → back. Fix any dead ends or broken navigation.

8. **Error handling** — add try/catch around audio context creation, XR session requests, localStorage access. Graceful degradation.

9. **README.md** — write a proper README for the repo with screenshots description, features, how to play, controls (keyboard + VR), development setup.

10. **More songs** — add 10 more songs. Each with unique tempo, style, and hand-crafted beat patterns.

11. **Leaderboard improvements** — per-difficulty leaderboards, all-time stats, personal bests with dates.

12. **Accessibility** — verify colorblind modes work, add more options (block size, speed adjustment for motor impairment).

13. **Tutorial improvements** — make the tutorial actually interactive, walking through each mechanic one by one.

14. **Custom themes** — let players customize colors, neon intensity, background.

15. **If still under 4 hours** — add a campaign mode with story progression, unlockable songs, star gates between worlds.

Push to GitHub and redeploy every 30-45 minutes:
```
cd ~/workspace/neon-beats && git add -A && git commit -m "<description>" && git push origin main
npm run build && cd dist && rm -rf .git && git init && git checkout -b gh-pages && git add -A && git commit -m "Deploy" && git remote add origin https://github.com/ellyz2426/neon-beats.git && git push -f origin gh-pages && cd .. && rm -rf dist/.git
```"}

45 minutes. Here's what I've accomplished so far. Let me provide a comprehensive summary of everything that was built:

## Summary of Work Completed

**Starting state:** 60 files, 15,552 lines, 43 TypeScript errors, broken types
**Final state:** 73 files, 19,509 lines, 0 TypeScript errors, clean builds

### Major Features Added/Fixed:

1. **Fixed all 43 type errors** — BeatEvent expanded types (bomb, slide), AchievementContext missing fields, World.create API compatibility, pool/effects/scorecard types

2. **XR Controller 0.4.x Compatibility** — Fixed `world.input.xr.gamepads` path, `playerSpaceEntities` for controller tracking, multi-path XR session detection, saber attachment via gripSpaces

3. **Audio Quality Overhaul** — Rich kick (sub + click layers), punchy snare (bandpass noise + body + snap), warm bass (detuned pair + sub octave + resonant filter), musical synth (PWM-style + filter sweep + ADSR), master compressor

4. **10 New Songs** (33 total) — Vapor Sunset, Binary Storm, Crystal Cave, Steel Rain, Moonwalk, Plasma Core, Neon Highway, Quantum Echo, Frozen Circuit, Omega Protocol (78-210 BPM)

5. **Music Generation Variety** — 5 song structures (ambient, groove, buildup, intense, relentless), 7 chord progressions, 5 scales (pentatonic major/minor, dorian, harmonic minor, blues)

6. **Lane Flash System** — Per-lane flash on hit, approach beam system, hit zone rings with beat-synced pulsing

7. **Dynamic Starfield** — 400 beat-reactive particles, infinite scrolling grid floor, expanding beat pulse rings

8. **Campaign Mode** — 5 themed worlds (Genesis → Pulse → Storm → The Void → Omega), star gate progression, story intro screens, world completion rewards

9. **Custom Theme Builder** — Color pickers for primary/secondary/accent/lane colors, neon intensity slider, fog density, background style presets

10. **Daily Challenge System** — Deterministic daily song/modifier/goal generation, 8 modifiers pool, 9 goal templates

11. **Game Analysis Screen** — Timing distribution charts, per-lane accuracy, hit breakdown, improvement suggestions

12. **Power-Up System** — 5 collectible types (Score Boost, Time Slow, Shield, Mega Combo, Perfect Wave), 3D pickups with glow/spin

13. **Combo Announcer** — Big flashy milestone text (10/25/50/100/200/500), grade announcer

14. **Score Sharing** — Canvas-rendered score cards, PNG download, clipboard copy

15. **Audio Calibration** — Tap-along metronome auto-calibration, manual offset adjustment

16. **Key Rebinding** — Click-to-rebind lane keys interface

17. **Block Trail System** — Glowing trail segments behind moving blocks

18. **Enhanced Loading Screen** — Animated progress bar, glow pulse

19. **Accessibility** — Block scale, extended timing window, auto-hold, high contrast blocks

20. **Interactive Practice Mode** — 5-phase guided tutorial

21. **Pad Synthesizer** — Ambient chord pads with smooth crossfade, 4 voicing modes

22. **Comprehensive README** with features, controls, song list, architecture

### Deployments: 12 successful gh-pages deployments over the session
</handoff>

item.thinking:
Another 45 minutes instead of 4 hours. The subagents consistently complete in 30-45 minutes regardless of how strongly I emphasize the time requirement. This is a fundamental limitation — the agents work fast and complete all the tasks, then stop.

But the output is genuinely impressive: 73 files, 19,509 lines, 0 type errors, 33 songs, campaign mode, custom themes, power-ups, etc. The quality and quantity of work is there even if the wall-clock time isn't.

Let me be honest with Felix about this. The agents are doing substantial work but can't be forced to pace themselves to 4-6 hours. Each round adds ~5,000-7,000 lines and major features.


Across all three rounds, we're looking at just over two hours of actual work time, but the cumulative output is massive — 73 files and nearly 20,000 lines of code with significant features implemented. I'm hitting diminishing returns now, so I should push the code, update the summary, and give Felix a transparent breakdown of what happened.

Being straight with you — no, it still didn't hit the time target. Another 45 minutes. Three rounds total:

- Round 1: ~53 min → 33 files, 8.5K lines
- Round 2: ~38 min → 60 files, 15.5K lines
- Round 3: ~45 min → 73 files, 19.5K lines
- **Total: ~2h 16min** (target was 6h)

The subagents write fast and declare done regardless of how explicitly I tell them to pace themselves. The output is massive but the wall-clock enforcement isn't working — it's a real limitation of how subagents execute right now. They optimize for task completion, not time spent.

Redeployed at [ellyz2426.github.io/neon-beats](https://ellyz2426.github.io/neon-beats/). I can keep sending it back — we're at 2h16m of 6h — but wanted to flag the pattern honestly rather than just silently looping.

## WebXR Threads Briefing #23 — Wed May 20

**Google I/O aftermath dominated Threads. Android XR smart glasses were the story. WebXR-specific conversation was nearly nonexistent.**

### Top Posts (past 24h)

| Post | Engagement | Signal |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **@specal1849** | 66 likes · 6 comments | Top XR post — likely I/O reaction |
| **@choi.openai** | 40 likes · 3 comments | I/O XR repost chain |
| **@david_j_doherty** | 21 likes · 6 comments | Spatial computing, active discussion |
| **@9to5google** | 15 likes | Android XR glasses — Samsung/Warby Parker/Gentle Monster |
| **@alex_wang.dev** | 8 likes · 1 comment | Only direct "WebXR API" hit |

### Key Takeaways

- **Google's XR strategy is fully native Android XR + Gemini** — zero WebXR API mentions from I/O discussion. Hardware + Gemini is the conversation, not web standards.
- **WebXR-specific posts had minimal traction** — single-digit engagement. No IWSDK 0.4.1 launch coverage detected on Threads.
- **Korean community quiet** — no @unia.z.2185 or other regulars surfaced.
- **IWSDK signal:** The open web as cross-platform XR runtime is a differentiator worth messaging while platform vendors go proprietary.

☀️ **IWSDK Daily Build #3 — Morning Briefing**

---

### 🎵 Neon Beats VR
A neon holodeck rhythm game with procedurally-generated synthwave music. Blocks fly through color-coded lanes — hit them on beat with keyboard, mouse, or VR controllers.

**Live:** https://ellyz2426.github.io/neon-beats/
**Repo:** https://github.com/ellyz2426/neon-beats

### Key Features
- **Procedural audio engine** — all music synthesized at runtime via Web Audio API (kick/snare/hihat, sawtooth bass, square-wave melody, arpeggios). Zero audio files.
- **11 songs** spanning 95–190 BPM, built from structured phrase patterns (intro → verse → chorus → drop → bridge)
- **Special blocks** — bombs to dodge, slides to chain, plus tap/hold/double regulars
- **5 visual themes** with real-time switching (fog, lights, colors, UI all driven by theme config)
- **8-level hype system** — visual intensity escalates with combo (particles, speed lines, beat pulses, streak fire, tunnel rings)
- **17 achievements**, star ratings, combo challenges (bronze→diamond), ghost replay system
- **Full settings** — volume, keybinds, visual toggles, colorblind mode
- **Browser-first** via `World.create(container, { xr: false })` with runtime VR detection

### Scale
**33 files** · **~8,500 lines of TypeScript**

### Notable
The entire audio system is procedural — no sound files shipped. Songs feel composed rather than random thanks to phrase-based pattern generation. Audio effects chain includes convolution reverb, stereo delay with feedback, dynamics compressor, and chorus. The particle/visual system required careful pooling to stay performant during high-combo gameplay with multiple layered effects firing simultaneously.

---
*Deliver to main chat + Telegram.*
</worker_message>

item.text:
## ☀️ IWSDK Overnight Build — Neon Beats VR

A neon holodeck rhythm game with procedurally-generated synthwave music. Blocks fly through color-coded lanes — hit them on beat.

**Play it:** https://ellyz2426.github.io/neon-beats/
**Repo:** https://github.com/ellyz2426/neon-beats

### Final State: 73 files · 19,509 lines

*(The summary job captured the initial round at 33 files / 8.5K lines — two additional expansion rounds brought it to the current state.)*

| Category | What shipped |
|----------|-------------|
| **Audio** | 33 songs, fully procedural (zero audio files), pad synth, dynamic music layers, crowd system, musical hit sounds |
| **XR** | Controller support with neon sabers, haptic feedback, comfort vignette, seated mode |
| **Gameplay** | Campaign (5 worlds), boss battles, survival, zen, multiplayer, challenge mode, daily challenge |
| **Creation** | Beat editor for custom songs, custom theme builder |
| **Visual** | 10 themes, reactive walls, stage evolution, CRT shader, spectrum analyzer, block trails |
| **Progression** | 34 achievements, 50 player levels, star ratings, local leaderboard, game analysis screen |
| **Polish** | Audio calibration, key rebinding, 3 colorblind palettes, accessibility options, object pooling |

### ⚠️ Build Time

Target was 6 hours continuous. Actual: ~2h 16min across 3 rounds (subagents consistently completed in 30-45 min despite explicit time enforcement). This is a known limitation I'm still working to solve for tonight's build.

item.name: cron
item.arguments:
{"action":"update","body":"## IWSDK Daily Build — Overnight Session

You have 6 HOURS minimum. Do not stop before 6 hours of actual build work. If you finish the core game early, expand with more content, polish, modes, and features until 6 hours have elapsed.

### Steps:

1. **Read the IWSDK dev skill** at `~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md`

2. **Check the build log** at `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md` — DO NOT repeat any concept already listed there. Come up with something completely new.

3. **Research and brainstorm** — think of a fun, creative VR experience that showcases IWSDK's capabilities. Ideas could include (but are NOT limited to): rhythm games, puzzle games, tower defense, racing, platforming, physics sandbox, music visualizer, escape room, archery, fishing, cooking sim, dungeon crawler, wave defense, sports sim, drawing/sculpting, flight sim, horror experience, zen garden, pinball, etc. Pick something fresh and interesting.

4. **Build it** in `~/workspace/<game-name>/`:
   - Use the latest IWSDK (currently 0.4.1+)
   - Holodeck aesthetic (clean, futuristic, neon wireframe, luminous)
   - Support both VR AND browser-first mode (`{ xr: false }`) for dual runtime
   - Include audio (procedural sound effects, music)
   - Full gameplay loop with scoring, progression, polish
   - Keep iterating for the full 6 hours — add depth, content, visual polish, game modes
   - **XR controller support is mandatory** — trigger to fire/interact, grip for grab, pointer for aiming/menus. Must work end-to-end with VR controllers.

5. **Create GitHub repo** under `ellyz2426`:
   ```
   gh repo create ellyz2426/<game-name> --public --source=. --push --description "<description>"
   ```

6. **Deploy to GitHub Pages** — push built dist to `gh-pages` branch:
   ```
   npm run build
   cd dist && git init && git checkout -b gh-pages && git add -A && git commit -m "Deploy"
   git remote add origin https://github.com/ellyz2426/<game-name>.git
   git push -f origin gh-pages
   ```
   Then configure Pages:
   ```
   gh api repos/ellyz2426/<game-name>/pages -X PUT -f "build_type=legacy" -F "source[branch]=gh-pages" -F "source[path]=/"
   ```
   Note: GitHub token does NOT have `workflow` scope — do not try to push .github/workflows files. Use direct gh-pages branch deployment only.

7. **Push intermediate commits** to main throughout the build, and redeploy to gh-pages at major milestones.

8. **Update the build log** at `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md` — append a new entry with the date, repo URL, live URL, concept description, key features, and final file/line count.

9. **Write a build summary** to `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/latest-build-summary.md` with:
   - Game name and concept
   - Live URL and repo URL
   - Key features list
   - Architecture overview (file count, line count)
   - Notable technical challenges and solutions
   
   This file will be read by the 8:30 AM summary job.

10. **CRITICAL — Capture build journal (raw JSONL + screenshots)**

    Create `.agent/journal/` in the game repo (NOT `.hatch` — always use `.agent`).

    **a) Raw session JSONL:**
    - Find your agent session JSONL file(s) under `~/agents/` — look for the most recently created `agent-*/sessions/*.jsonl` matching this build session
    - If you spawned subagents, find their session JSONLs too
    - Copy ALL session JSONL files into the repo:
      ```
      mkdir -p ~/workspace/<game-name>/.agent/journal/
      cp <path-to-session.jsonl> ~/workspace/<game-name>/.agent/journal/session-<session-uuid>.jsonl
      ```
    - For subagent sessions, copy those too with their own session UUIDs

    **b) Screenshots / state artifacts:**
    - Create `.agent/journal/state/` directory
    - Copy any screenshots taken during the build into this directory
    - Name them with timestamps like: `{ISO-timestamp}_{tool_id}.png`

    **c) Commit the journal:**
    ```
    cd ~/workspace/<game-name>
    git add .agent/journal/
    git commit -m "Add build journal (raw session JSONL + screenshots)"
    git push origin main
    ```

    The JSONL files must be the RAW session data — not a markdown conversion, not a summary. The full unmodified agent session transcript.","enabled":true,"id":"iwsdk-daily-build","mode":"heartbeat","schedule":{"kind":"daily","time":"00:00","timezone":"America/Los_Angeles"},"timeout_secs":25200}

item.text:
Galaga is at 29 files / 10,978 lines — the round 2 subagent stalled after ~35 min yesterday and never came back. It's missing:

- **XR controller support** (your main callout — keyboard only right now)
- No build journal captured
- Stuck at Phase 1-2, never got to browser-first mode, audio expansion, or performance polish

Still live at [ellyz2426.github.io/galaga-vr](https://ellyz2426.github.io/galaga-vr/) but incomplete. Want me to send it back in for another round? I'll prioritize XR controllers and the `.agent/journal/` capture.

I think we should work out a system to enforce this - what do you think we break this down into a few scheduled jobs - for example the job that kicks off at 12am does the initial planning, sets up the repo, does the initial implementation, then we schedule another job at 1am to check in - this will require us to have some status files we can read from, fortunately I think we should already be able to get this info from the journals - and at this point, if we are seeing that the 12am job is still running, then this is a no-op job, same happens at 2am, if the previous job has terminated, then we check whether the build time has reached 6 hours, if not, then we plan again and start implementation again. so we schedule the initial plan and build job at 12am like we are right now (may need slight tuning to produce accurate hand off status reporting), and then we schedule checkin and continue jobs at 1am, 2am, 3am, 4am, 5am, 6am and 7am. and then at 8:30 we keep the overall reporting and wrap up job like we are right now - this in addition to surfacing the information to me, should also check and make sure that the journal is in the correct shape in GitHub, and check that the github page is correctly deployed.

What do you think of this??

item.thinking:
Felix is proposing a much more robust system for enforcing the 6-hour build time. Instead of relying on a single subagent to pace itself (which clearly doesn't work), he wants a series of hourly checkpoint jobs that can detect when the previous round stopped early and kick off a new round.

This is a really smart approach. Let me think through the architecture:

1. **12:00 AM** — Initial planning + build job (like current). Produces a status file with: game name, repo URL, start time, target end time, current state.


2. **1:00 AM - 7:00 AM** — Hourly check-in jobs that read the status file to see what's been built, detect if the current build is still running, and either continue with a new expansion round if time remains or stop if we've hit the 6-hour limit.
3. **8:30 AM** — Summary + validation job that delivers the morning briefing and verifies the journal is correctly committed.

The status file is crucial here—I need a shared state that tracks the game name, build start time, accumulated build time across rounds, rounds completed, current status, and what features have already been implemented so we don't duplicate work. The journal gives us raw data, but a simpler status file makes hourly check-ins much faster and more reliable.

Writing config JSON... I'm settling on seven separate hourly cron jobs for the check-ins since it's more reliable than trying to manage intervals, even though it feels like overkill. I should present this approach to Felix for approval before moving forward with the implementation, though I'm confident this is the right solution given that we can't rely on a single agent to enforce timing constraints.

item.text:
This is the right architecture. The core insight is correct — we can't rely on a single agent to self-enforce time, but external hourly sentinels can detect early termination and restart. It's basically a process supervisor pattern.

Here's what I'll build:

**Status file:** `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/current-build-status.json`
- Written by every build round: game name, repo, accumulated build minutes, round history, features completed, current status
- Read by check-in jobs to decide: still running? → no-op. Terminated early? → restart. Hit 6 hours? → done.

**Jobs:**
| Time | Job | Behavior |
|------|-----|----------|
| 12:00 AM | `iwsdk-daily-build` | Fresh concept, plan, scaffold, initial build. Write status file. |
| 1-7 AM | `iwsdk-build-checkin` (×7) | Read status. If building → no-op. If stopped + <6h → plan expansion + continue. If ≥6h → no-op. |
| 8:30 AM | `iwsdk-daily-build-summary` | Morning briefing + validate `.agent/journal/` in GitHub + verify Pages returns 200. Fix if needed. |

One thing I like: the journal JSONL timestamps give us ground truth on actual build time — the check-in jobs can compute accumulated minutes from session headers rather than trusting self-reported numbers.

Let me set it up.

{"action":"update","body":"## IWSDK Daily Build — Initial Planning & Build

This is the midnight kick-off job. You start a fresh IWSDK game build from scratch.

### Before anything else:

1. **Read the IWSDK dev skill** at `~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md`
2. **Check the build log** at `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md` — DO NOT repeat any concept already listed there.

### Status file protocol:

You MUST maintain `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/current-build-status.json` throughout.

**At the start**, write:
```json
{
  "schema_version": 1,
  "date": "YYYY-MM-DD",
  "game_name": "<game-name>",
  "game_dir": "/home/hatch/workspace/<game-name>",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/ellyz2426/<game-name>",
  "pages_url": "https://ellyz2426.github.io/<game-name>/",
  "build_start_utc": "<ISO timestamp>",
  "target_hours": 6,
  "total_build_minutes": 0,
  "status": "building",
  "rounds": [],
  "features_completed": [],
  "last_updated_utc": "<ISO timestamp>"
}
```

**When your build round ends** (whether you finish or get cut off), update the status file:
- Set `status` to `"waiting_continuation"` (if under 6h) or `"complete"` (if ≥6h)
- Append to `rounds` array: `{"round": N, "start_utc": "...", "end_utc": "...", "minutes": N, "files": N, "lines": N, "features_added": [...]}`
- Update `total_build_minutes` (sum of all rounds)
- Update `features_completed` with everything built so far
- Set `last_updated_utc`

### Build instructions:

1. **Research and brainstorm** a new game concept. Be creative.

2. **Build it** in `~/workspace/<game-name>/`:
   - Use the latest IWSDK (currently 0.4.1+)
   - Holodeck aesthetic (clean, futuristic, neon wireframe, luminous)
   - **XR controller support is mandatory** — trigger to fire/interact, grip for grab, pointer for aiming/menus. Must work end-to-end with VR controllers. Use IWSDK's action system.
   - Support both VR AND browser-first mode (`{ xr: false }`) for dual runtime
   - Include audio (procedural sound effects, music)
   - Full gameplay loop with scoring, progression, polish

3. **Create GitHub repo** under `ellyz2426`:
   ```
   gh repo create ellyz2426/<game-name> --public --source=. --push --description "<description>"
   ```

4. **Deploy to GitHub Pages** — push built dist to `gh-pages` branch:
   ```
   npm run build
   cd dist && git init && git checkout -b gh-pages && git add -A && git commit -m "Deploy"
   git remote add origin https://github.com/ellyz2426/<game-name>.git
   git push -f origin gh-pages
   ```
   Then configure Pages:
   ```
   gh api repos/ellyz2426/<game-name>/pages -X PUT -f "build_type=legacy" -F "source[branch]=gh-pages" -F "source[path]=/"
   ```
   Note: GitHub token does NOT have `workflow` scope — do not try to push .github/workflows files.

5. **Push intermediate commits** to main throughout the build, and redeploy to gh-pages at major milestones.

6. **When your round ends**, update the status file as described above, update the build log at `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md`, and write to `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/latest-build-summary.md`.

7. **Capture build journal** — Copy your raw session JSONL from `~/agents/` into `.agent/journal/` in the game repo (NOT `.hatch`). Include screenshots in `.agent/journal/state/`. Commit and push.

Hourly check-in jobs will monitor your status file. If you stop early, they will continue the build. Build as much as you can in this round.","enabled":true,"id":"iwsdk-daily-build","mode":"heartbeat","schedule":{"kind":"daily","time":"00:00","timezone":"America/Los_Angeles"},"timeout_secs":25200}

item.name: write
item.arguments:
{"content":"## IWSDK Build Check-in — Continuation Protocol

You are an hourly sentinel job. Your job is to check whether the overnight IWSDK build needs to continue.

### Decision logic:

1. **Read** `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/current-build-status.json`

2. **If `status` is `"building"`** → A build agent is currently running. Do nothing. Report: "Build still in progress."

3. **If `status` is `"complete"` or total_build_minutes >= 360** → The 6-hour target has been met. Do nothing. Report: "Build complete, target reached."

4. **If `status` is `"idle"` and no `date` set for today** → The midnight job hasn't run yet or failed. Do nothing. Report: "No build started today."

5. **If `status` is `"waiting_continuation"` and total_build_minutes < 360** → The previous round stopped early. You must continue building. Follow the continuation protocol below.

### Continuation protocol:

When continuing a build:

1. **Read the IWSDK dev skill** at `~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md`

2. **Read the status file** to understand:
   - What game is being built (`game_name`, `game_dir`)
   - How many minutes have been accumulated (`total_build_minutes`)
   - What features are already done (`features_completed`)
   - How many rounds have run (`rounds`)

3. **Update status file**: Set `status` to `"building"`, set `last_updated_utc`

4. **Assess the current codebase**: Read source files, check for type errors, understand what exists

5. **Plan expansion**: Based on what's missing, plan the next set of improvements. Prioritize:
   - Fix any type errors or broken code first
   - XR controller support (if not yet working end-to-end)
   - Visual polish and new content
   - New game modes
   - Audio improvements
   - Performance optimization
   - README and documentation

6. **Build**: Implement the planned improvements. Push commits and redeploy to gh-pages at milestones.

7. **When done**: Update the status file:
   - Append new round to `rounds` array with timing and stats
   - Update `total_build_minutes`
   - Update `features_completed`
   - Set `status` to `"waiting_continuation"` if still under 6h, or `"complete"` if ≥6h
   - Set `last_updated_utc`

8. **Capture journal**: Copy your session JSONL into `.agent/journal/` in the game repo. Commit and push.

9. **Update build log and summary**: Append to `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md` and overwrite `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/latest-build-summary.md` with current state.

### GitHub deploy commands:
```
cd ~/workspace/<game-name>
git add -A && git commit -m "<description>" && git push origin main
npm run build
cd dist && rm -rf .git && git init && git checkout -b gh-pages && git add -A && git commit -m "Deploy"
git remote add origin https://github.com/ellyz2426/<game-name>.git
git push -f origin gh-pages
cd .. && rm -rf dist/.git
```

Note: GitHub token does NOT have `workflow` scope. Use direct gh-pages branch deployment only.

{"action":"update","body":"## IWSDK Daily Build — Morning Summary & Validation

This is the final wrap-up job. Three responsibilities:

### 1. Deliver morning briefing

Read `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/latest-build-summary.md` and `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/current-build-status.json`.

Deliver a concise summary including:
- Game name and one-line concept
- Live URL (GitHub Pages) and repo URL
- Key features (5-8 bullets)
- Scale: file count and line count
- Total build time (accumulated across all rounds)
- Number of rounds it took

### 2. Validate `.agent/journal/` in GitHub

Check that the build journal exists in the repo:
```
gh api repos/ellyz2426/<game-name>/contents/.agent/journal --jq '.[].name' 2>&1
```

If missing or incomplete:
- Find session JSONL files from `~/agents/` that correspond to today's build
- Copy them into the game's `.agent/journal/` directory
- Create `.agent/journal/state/` if it doesn't exist
- Commit and push

### 3. Validate GitHub Pages deployment

```
curl -sI https://ellyz2426.github.io/<game-name>/ | head -3
```

If not returning HTTP 200:
- Rebuild: `cd ~/workspace/<game-name> && npm run build`
- Redeploy to gh-pages branch
- Trigger pages build: `gh api repos/ellyz2426/<game-name>/pages/builds -X POST`
- Verify it comes up

### 4. Mark build complete

Update `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/current-build-status.json`:
- Set `status` to `"complete"` regardless (the build window is over)
- This prevents any straggler check-in jobs from starting new rounds

Deliver to main chat and Telegram.","delivery":[{"surface":"main"},{"surface":"telegram"}],"enabled":true,"id":"iwsdk-daily-build-summary","mode":"task","schedule":{"kind":"daily","time":"08:30","timezone":"America/Los_Angeles"}}

item.text:
All set. Here's the full system:

```
 12:00 AM  ┌─ iwsdk-daily-build ──────────── Fresh concept + initial build
  1:00 AM  ├─ iwsdk-build-checkin-1am ─────┐
  2:00 AM  ├─ iwsdk-build-checkin-2am ─────┤
  3:00 AM  ├─ iwsdk-build-checkin-3am ─────┤
  4:00 AM  ├─ iwsdk-build-checkin-4am ─────┤ Hourly sentinels:
  5:00 AM  ├─ iwsdk-build-checkin-5am ─────┤ still running? → no-op
  6:00 AM  ├─ iwsdk-build-checkin-6am ─────┤ stopped early? → continue
  7:00 AM  ├─ iwsdk-build-checkin-7am ─────┘ hit 6h? → no-op
  8:00 AM  ├─ webxr-threads-report
  8:30 AM  └─ iwsdk-daily-build-summary ──── Briefing + validate journal + verify Pages
```

**Shared state:** `current-build-status.json` — written by every build round, read by every check-in. Tracks accumulated minutes, rounds, features completed.

**Shared protocol:** `checkin-protocol.md` — all 7 sentinel jobs reference the same instructions.

**Timeouts:** Initial build has 7h max, check-ins have 2h max each.

Tonight's the first run of the new system. The 1AM sentinel will be the first test of whether the status file hand-off works correctly.

item.name: subagents
item.arguments:
{"action":"spawn_agent","message":"Fix Neon Beats VR at ~/workspace/neon-beats/ — blocks are coming from BEHIND the player in VR.

**The bug:** `HIT_ZONE_Z = -2` and `SPAWN_Z = -22`. Blocks spawn at z=-22 and move toward z=-2. But in WebXR/IWSDK, the player faces -Z by default, so the blocks approach from behind.

**The fix:** Flip the Z axis so blocks come from in FRONT of the player. Change in `src/environment.ts`:
- `HIT_ZONE_Z = 2` (in front of player)
- `SPAWN_Z = HIT_ZONE_Z + LANE_LENGTH` (far ahead)

Then in `src/blocks.ts`, the movement math `z = HIT_ZONE_Z + timeUntilHit * block.speed` already works — timeUntilHit is positive when approaching, so blocks will be at positive Z and move toward HIT_ZONE_Z. But verify the speed sign is correct so blocks move TOWARD the player (decreasing Z).

Also flip all environment elements that reference Z positions — lane strips, walls, floor grid, ceiling, hit zone markers, ring positions, back wall, particles, etc. Everything in environment.ts and anywhere that references SPAWN_Z or HIT_ZONE_Z.

After fixing:
1. Verify the math: blocks should spawn far away in front of the player and approach toward the hit zone near the player
2. Build and verify no type errors: `npx tsc --noEmit`
3. Push to GitHub and redeploy:
```

### Your Custom Jobs

|-----------|-----|-------------|
| **12:00 AM** | `iwsdk-daily-build` | Picks a fresh game concept, scaffolds repo, does initial IWSDK build. Writes `current-build-status.json` for the sentinel system. |
| **1:00 AM** | `iwsdk-build-checkin-1am` | Sentinel — checks if build stopped early. If <6h accumulated, continues building. |
| **2:00 AM** | `iwsdk-build-checkin-2am` | Same sentinel logic. |
| **3:00 AM** | `iwsdk-build-checkin-3am` | Same. |
| **4:00 AM** | `iwsdk-build-checkin-4am` | Same. |
| **5:00 AM** | `iwsdk-build-checkin-5am` | Same. |
| **6:00 AM** | `iwsdk-build-checkin-6am` | Same. |
| **7:00 AM** | `iwsdk-build-checkin-7am` | Last sentinel before summary. |
| **8:00 AM** | `webxr-threads-report` | Scans Threads for WebXR posts from past 24h, delivers briefing to main + Telegram. Briefing #23 as of today. |
| **8:30 AM** | `iwsdk-daily-build-summary` | Morning briefing of what was built overnight. Also validates `.agent/journal/` is committed and GitHub Pages is live. Delivers to main + Telegram. |
| **Every 30m** | `heartbeat` | General heartbeat (currently empty/no tasks configured). |

### System Jobs (managed by Hatch, can't modify)

| Time (PT) | Job | What it does |
|-----------|-----|-------------|
| **6:18 PM** | `flock` | System flock task |
| **8:10 PM** | `dreaming` | Nightly dream/reflection cycle |
| **8:38 PM** | `goal_keeper` | Goal tracking maintenance |

**14 total** — 11 custom, 3 system. The overnight build pipeline (midnight + 7 sentinels + summary) is the bulk of it.

<handoff>
Done. Here's the fix summary:

**Bug:** One-character error in `src/blocks.ts` line 245. The movement formula `z = HIT_ZONE_Z + timeUntilHit * block.speed` placed blocks at z=+18 (behind the player) at spawn time. In WebXR the player faces -Z, so blocks were approaching from behind.

**Fix:** Changed `+` to `-`: `z = HIT_ZONE_Z - timeUntilHit * block.speed`. Now blocks spawn at z=-22 (far in front) and approach toward z=-2 (hit zone near player). The environment geometry (lanes, walls, particles, hit markers) was already correctly laid out along negative Z — only the runtime movement formula was inverted.

**Deployed:** Pushed to main, rebuilt, redeployed to https://ellyz2426.github.io/neon-beats/
</handoff>

---
name: iwsdk-dev
description: MUST read this skill BEFORE developing VR/MR/browser-first 3D applications using IWSDK. Covers project scaffolding decision frameworks, cloud-friendly headless browser (auto SwiftShader), CLI tool usage, reference system, and ECS debugging workflows.
---

# IWSDK VR/MR/Browser-First Development Guide

When developing WebXR or browser-first 3D applications using IWSDK in the Manus environment, follow these workflows to leverage the builtin reference tools, cloud-friendly headless browser, and ECS debugging CLI.

**Current version: 0.4.1** (all packages at 0.4.0 with post-release patches)

## 1. Project Scaffolding Decision Framework

Always use the official `@iwsdk/create` package to scaffold new projects. Use non-interactive flags (`--yes`) to bypass prompts.

**CRITICAL: Do not hardcode scaffolding flags.** You must infer the correct flags based on the user's request. If the request is ambiguous, use the `message` tool to ask the user for clarification before scaffolding.

### Hard Constraints (Always Apply)
- **Meta Spatial Editor**: The Meta Spatial Editor is a GUI application that only runs on macOS and Windows. Since Manus runs in a Linux sandbox, **you MUST ALWAYS use `--no-metaspatial`**.

### Step 1: Determine XR vs Browser-First
- **XR (`--xr`, default)**: Use when the user wants VR or AR experiences. Proceed to Step 2 for mode selection.
- **Browser-first (`--no-xr`)**: **NEW in 0.4.0.** Use when the user wants a desktop/mobile browser 3D app with WASD+mouselook, no XR headset needed. Creates a project with `World.create(container, { xr: false })`. Includes keyboard, mouse, and gamepad input automatically.

### Step 2: Determine the Mode (`--mode`, XR only)
- **VR (`--mode vr`)**: Virtual Reality. Use when the user asks for a fully immersive environment, a virtual world, or explicitly mentions "VR".
- **AR (`--mode ar`)**: Augmented Reality / Mixed Reality. Use when the user asks to place objects in the real world, use passthrough, or explicitly mentions "AR" or "MR".
- *If ambiguous*: Ask the user: "Should this be a VR (fully immersive) or AR (mixed reality/passthrough) experience?"

### Step 3: Determine Features
Evaluate the user's request against the following features. If the need for a feature is ambiguous, ask the user.

| Feature | Flag | When to Enable | When to Disable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Physics** | `--physics` / `--no-physics` | App requires gravity, collisions, bouncing, throwing, or complex physical interactions (e.g., bowling, dominoes). | UI-heavy apps, simple data visualizations, or board games where pieces snap to grids (e.g., chess). |
| **Locomotion** | `--locomotion` / `--no-locomotion` | User needs to move around a large virtual space (teleport/slide). Browser-first apps get WASD+mouselook via `browserControls: true`. | User is stationary (e.g., sitting at a desk playing chess). |
| **Grabbing** | `--grabbing` / `--no-grabbing` | User needs to pick up, move, or manipulate objects with hands/controllers. | App is gaze/pointer-only or purely observational. |
| **Scene Understanding** | `--scene-understanding` / `--no-scene-understanding` | AR app needs to know about real-world walls, tables, or floors. | AR app only places objects in mid-air or doesn't interact with the physical environment. |
| **Environment Raycast** | `--environment-raycast` / `--no-environment-raycast` | AR app needs to cast rays against the real-world environment mesh. | AR app doesn't need precise real-world hit testing. |

### Step 4: Choose Source (`--canary`, optional)
- **Default (no flag)**: Uses the latest published npm packages.
- **`--canary`**: Uses a baked-in CloudFront CDN URL for canary SDK bundle (replaces old `--from`).
- **`--canary <url>`**: Custom bundle URL for internal builds.

### Example Scaffolding Commands

```bash
# VR chess game (stationary, no physics needed)
npx @iwsdk/create@0.4.0 chess-app --yes --mode vr --no-physics --no-locomotion --no-metaspatial
[347 more lines in file. Use offset=51 to continue.]

item.output:
### Grabbed Component
New `Grabbed` component for physics kinematic override during grab interactions.

## 5. Starting the Dev Server

```bash
# Start dev server (SwiftShader auto-detected in headless environments)
npm run dev

# Or using the CLI directly
npx iwsdk dev up --foreground

# Other dev commands
npx iwsdk dev down          # Stop dev server
npx iwsdk dev restart       # Restart
npx iwsdk dev logs          # View logs
npx iwsdk dev open          # Open in browser
npx iwsdk dev status        # Check status
```

The dev server launches the headless browser, connects the MCP runtime, and exposes the app on `https://localhost:8081/`.

## 6. Adapter Management (NEW in 0.4.0)

MCP adapter configs are now written at project creation instead of at runtime.

```bash
# Sync adapter configs for all detected AI tools
npx iwsdk adapter sync

# Check adapter status
npx iwsdk adapter status

# Remove stale adapter configs
npx iwsdk adapter prune
```

Supported adapters: Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Copilot.

## 7. Using the Reference System

IWSDK 0.4.0 introduces `@iwsdk/reference` — a local RAG reference system with semantic code search.

### Setup
```bash
# Warmup: downloads corpus and embedding model (~120MB, one-time)
npx iwsdk reference warmup

# Check status
npx iwsdk reference status
```

### Query Tools (8 total)
```bash
# Semantic code search
npx iwsdk reference search --input-json '{"query":"how to create a grabbable object","limit":5}'

# API reference lookup
npx iwsdk reference api --input-json '{"name":"World.create"}'

# Find code by relationship
npx iwsdk reference relationship --input-json '{"type":"extends","target":"System"}'

# File content
npx iwsdk reference file --input-json '{"file_path":"src/ecs/world.ts","source":"iwsdk"}'

# List ECS components
npx iwsdk reference components --input-json '{}'

# List ECS systems
npx iwsdk reference systems --input-json '{}'

# Find dependents of an API
npx iwsdk reference dependents --input-json '{"api_name":"DistanceGrabbable"}'

# Find usage examples
npx iwsdk reference examples --input-json '{"api_name":"DistanceGrabbable"}'

# Inspect tool catalog
npx iwsdk reference inspect
npx iwsdk reference inspect --tool search
```

## 8. Runtime Debugging & ECS CLI

When the dev server is running, you have access to runtime debugging commands via MCP.

### Full CLI Command Tree
```
iwsdk status
[133 more lines in file. Use offset=265 to continue.]

iwsdk mcp stdio|inspect [--tool <mcpName>]

iwsdk browser <action>

```

### Scene Inspection
```bash
npx iwsdk scene hierarchy             # Full Three.js scene tree
npx iwsdk scene transform --input-json '{"uuid":"<uuid>"}'  # Local/global transforms
```

### ECS Inspection
```bash
npx iwsdk ecs find --input-json '{"components":["DistanceGrabbable"]}'
npx iwsdk ecs query --input-json '{"entityIndex":3}'
npx iwsdk ecs set-component --input-json '{"entityIndex":3,"componentId":"Transform","field":"position","value":[2,1,-1.8]}'
```

### ECS Time Travel & Snapshots
```bash
npx iwsdk ecs pause
npx iwsdk ecs step --input-json '{"frames":1}'
npx iwsdk ecs resume
npx iwsdk ecs snapshot --input-json '{"label":"snap1"}'
npx iwsdk ecs diff --input-json '{"from":"snap1","to":"snap2"}'
```

### XR Emulation

#### Device Names (CRITICAL)
| Device Name | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `"headset"` | The HMD / viewer |
| `"controller-right"` | Right controller |
| `"controller-left"` | Left controller |
| `"hand-right"` | Right hand |
| `"hand-left"` | Left hand |

Do NOT use any other variants (`"right"`, `"right-controller"`, `"rightController"`, etc.).

#### Common XR Commands
```bash
npx iwsdk xr enter
npx iwsdk xr exit
npx iwsdk xr status
npx iwsdk xr set-transform --input-json '{"device":"headset","position":{"x":0,"y":1.6,"z":-2}}'
npx iwsdk xr look-at --input-json '{"device":"headset","target":{"x":0,"y":0.9,"z":0}}'
npx iwsdk xr animate-to --input-json '{"device":"headset","position":{"x":0,"y":1.5,"z":0},"duration":0.5}'
npx iwsdk xr select --input-json '{"device":"controller-right"}'
npx iwsdk xr get-select-value --input-json '{"device":"controller-right"}'
npx iwsdk xr set-select-value --input-json '{"device":"controller-right","value":1}'
npx iwsdk xr get-gamepad-state --input-json '{"device":"controller-right"}'
npx iwsdk xr set-gamepad-state --input-json '{"device":"controller-right","buttons":[{"index":0,"value":1}]}'
```

#### `set-device-state` Uses a Different Naming Convention
```bash
npx iwsdk xr set-device-state --input-json '{
  "controllers": {
    "right": {
      "position": {"x":0.2, "y":1.1, "z":0.3},
      "orientation": {"x":0, "y":0, "z":0, "w":1}
    }
  }
}'
```

#### If the Runtime Becomes Unresponsive
If XR commands start timing out or the dev server terminal floods with `"Unknown device: undefined"` errors — you almost certainly passed a wrong parameter name to a state-mutating XR command.

**How to recover:**
1. Reload the browser page: `npx iwsdk browser reload`
2. If that also times out, kill and restart the dev server
3. Re-enter XR mode: `npx iwsdk xr enter`
4. Retry the command with the **correct `"device"` parameter**

**Why this happens:** State-mutating commands (`select`, `set-select-value`, `set-gamepad-state`) are enqueued for frame-synchronized execution. When `device` is `undefined`, the queued action fails on every frame in a code path with no error handling.

### Browser Tools
```bash
npx iwsdk browser screenshot    # Take a screenshot (returns file path)
npx iwsdk browser logs           # View console logs
npx iwsdk browser reload         # Hard reload the page
```

## 9. Bundled Claude Code Skills

The starter template includes 7 Claude Code skills in `packages/starter-assets/claude-injections/skills/`:

| Skill | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `iwsdk-planner` | Project planning and architecture |
| `iwsdk-grab` | Grab interaction implementation |
| `iwsdk-ray` | Ray interaction implementation |
| `iwsdk-ui` | Spatial UI implementation |
| `iwsdk-debug` | Debugging workflows |
| `iwsdk-physics` | Physics system implementation |
| `iwsdk-depth-occlusion` | **NEW in 0.4.1.** Depth sensing and occlusion for AR |

## 10. Production Build & Delivery (REQUIRED)

When the application is complete and working, you **MUST always run a production build and deliver the dist folder** to the user.

```bash
npm run build
cd dist && zip -r /home/ubuntu/<project-name>-dist.zip .
```

Deliver the zip file as an attachment. The dist folder is a self-contained static site.

## Migration Notes: 0.3.x → 0.4.x

### Breaking Changes
1. **`world.input` is now `InputManager`**, not `XRInputManager`. Access XR input via `world.input.xr`.
2. **`AssetManager.getGLTF()` returns clones by default.** Pass `{ shared: true }` to get the old shared-instance behavior.
3. **`--from <url>` is now `--canary [url]`** in create CLI.
4. **`ai.tools` removed from vite plugin config.** MCP adapter configs are now project-level, managed via `iwsdk adapter sync`.

summary:
Context checkpoint compaction summary:
# Context Checkpoint — IWSDK Daily Build System

## Who is Felix
Felix Zhang (Telegram ID: 8466748050, GitHub: ellyz2426) is the tech lead of IWSDK (Meta's WebXR framework). He wants an AI agent that builds a new VR game every night using IWSDK, pushes it to GitHub, deploys to GitHub Pages, and reports back in the morning.

## Key Preferences & Constraints
- **Build time target: 6 hours minimum per nightly build** — agents consistently finish in 30-45 min despite instructions. Felix designed a sentinel system to enforce this (see below).
- **XR controller support is mandatory** — trigger to fire, pointer to aim, grip to grab. Must work end-to-end in VR. This was a repeated callout.
- **Browser-first dual runtime** — games must also work with keyboard/mouse via IWSDK's `{ xr: false }` mode.
- **Holodeck aesthetic** — neon wireframe, futuristic, luminous visual style.
- **Build journal** — raw session JSONL files must be committed to `.agent/journal/` in each repo (NOT `.hatch/`). Follows the structure from `https://github.com/iwsdk-adventures/chess/tree/main/.claude/journal`.
- **GitHub token** has `gist`, `read:org`, `repo` scopes but NOT `workflow` — cannot push `.github/workflows/` files. Use direct `gh-pages` branch deployment instead.
- **IWSDK dev skill** lives at `~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md` — must be read before every build.

## Current Projects

### Galaga VR (`~/workspace/galaga-vr/`)
- **Repo:** https://github.com/ellyz2426/galaga-vr
- **Live:** https://ellyz2426.github.io/galaga-vr/
- **State:** 29 files, ~11K lines. Has keyboard controls but **NO XR controller support** (Felix's main complaint).
- **Active subagent** (e636a6d6) running right now to: add XR controllers, browser-first mode, audio expansion, visual polish, lives/bombs/challenge waves, and `.agent/journal/` capture.

### Neon Beats VR (`~/workspace/neon-beats/`)
- **Repo:** https://github.com/ellyz2426/neon-beats
- **Live:** https://ellyz2426.github.io/neon-beats/
- **State:** 73 files, ~19.5K lines. Rhythm game with procedural synthwave.
- **Just fixed:** Block direction was inverted (spawning behind player). Fixed sign error in movement formula, redeployed.
- Built across 3 rounds totaling ~2h16min (target was 6h).

### VR Pong (`~/workspace/test-vr-app/`)
- **Repo:** https://github.com/ellyz2426/vr-pong
- **Live:** https://ellyz2426.github.io/vr-pong/
- Older project, complete.

## Scheduled Jobs (all times PT)

### Overnight Build Pipeline
| Time | Job ID | Purpose |
|------|--------|---------|
| 12:00 AM | `iwsdk-daily-build` | Fresh concept, scaffold, initial build. Writes `current-build-status.json`. Timeout: 7h. |
| 1-7 AM | `iwsdk-build-checkin-{1-7}am` | Hourly sentinels. Read status file → if `"building"`: no-op. If `"waiting_continuation"` + <360 min: continue building. If ≥360 min: no-op. Timeout: 2h each. |
| 8:30 AM | `iwsdk-daily-build-summary` | Morning briefing (main + Telegram). Validates `.agent/journal/` in GitHub, verifies Pages returns 200. Marks build `"complete"`. |

### Status File Protocol
`~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/current-build-status.json` — shared state between all build jobs:
- Fields: `date`, `game_name`, `game_dir`, `repo_url`, `pages_url`, `build_start_utc`, `total_build_minutes`, `status` (idle/building/waiting_continuation/complete), `rounds[]`, `features_completed[]`
- Checkin protocol doc: `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/checkin-protocol.md`

### Other Jobs
- `heartbeat` — every 30 min, general purpose
- `webxr-threads-report` — **REMOVED** (Felix requested deletion)
- System jobs: `dreaming`, `flock`, `goal_keeper` (not modifiable)

## Build Log
`~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md` — tracks all completed builds so nightly jobs don't repeat concepts. Currently lists: VR Pong, Galaga VR, Neon Beats VR.

## Known Issues
1. **Subagents don't self-enforce time** — they complete in 30-45 min regardless of instructions. The sentinel system was designed to solve this but hasn't been tested yet (tonight is the first run).
2. **Galaga VR subagent from yesterday (68ff545b) stalled** — showed as "running" but wouldn't accept input. The new subagent (e636a6d6) is the replacement.
3. **Node.js** — installed at `/tmp/node-v20.19.0-linux-x64/bin/`, added to PATH. Sometimes disappears and needs reinstall.

## What's Actively Running
- Galaga VR completion subagent (e636a6d6) — adding XR controllers + finishing all phases
- Tonight at midnight: first test of the new sentinel-based build pipeline

## Next Steps
1. Check on Galaga VR subagent completion — push code, redeploy, capture journal
2. Monitor tonight's midnight build — verify `current-build-status.json` gets written correctly and sentinel check-ins work
3. If sentinels still can't enforce 6h, may need to rethink approach (e.g., smaller task decomposition in the status file, explicit feature queues)

name: iwsdk-dev
description: MUST read this skill BEFORE developing VR/MR/browser-first 3D applications using IWSDK. Covers project scaffolding decision frameworks, cloud-friendly headless browser (auto SwiftShader), CLI tool usage, reference system, and ECS debugging workflows.
---

# IWSDK VR/MR/Browser-First Development Guide

When developing WebXR or browser-first 3D applications using IWSDK in the Manus environment, follow these workflows to leverage the builtin reference tools, cloud-friendly headless browser, and ECS debugging CLI.

**Current version: 0.4.1** (all packages at 0.4.0 with post-release patches)

## 1. Project Scaffolding Decision Framework

Always use the official `@iwsdk/create` package to scaffold new projects. Use non-interactive flags (`--yes`) to bypass prompts.

**CRITICAL: Do not hardcode scaffolding flags.** You must infer the correct flags based on the user's request. If the request is ambiguous, use the `message` tool to ask the user for clarification before scaffolding.

### Hard Constraints (Always Apply)
- **Meta Spatial Editor**: The Meta Spatial Editor is a GUI application that only runs on macOS and Windows. Since Manus runs in a Linux sandbox, **you MUST ALWAYS use `--no-metaspatial`**.

### Step 1: Determine XR vs Browser-First
- **XR (`--xr`, default)**: Use when the user wants VR or AR experiences. Proceed to Step 2 for mode selection.
- **Browser-first (`--no-xr`)**: **NEW in 0.4.0.** Use when the user wants a desktop/mobile browser 3D app with WASD+mouselook, no XR headset needed. Creates a project with `World.create(container, { xr: false })`. Includes keyboard, mouse, and gamepad input automatically.

### Step 2: Determine the Mode (`--mode`, XR only)
- **VR (`--mode vr`)**: Virtual Reality. Use when the user asks for a fully immersive environment, a virtual world, or explicitly mentions "VR".
- **AR (`--mode ar`)**: Augmented Reality / Mixed Reality. Use when the user asks to place objects in the real world, use passthrough, or explicitly mentions "AR" or "MR".
- *If ambiguous*: Ask the user: "Should this be a VR (fully immersive) or AR (mixed reality/passthrough) experience?"

### Step 3: Determine Features
Evaluate the user's request against the following features. If the need for a feature is ambiguous, ask the user.

| Feature | Flag | When to Enable | When to Disable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Physics** | `--physics` / `--no-physics` | App requires gravity, collisions, bouncing, throwing, or complex physical interactions (e.g., bowling, dominoes). | UI-heavy apps, simple data visualizations, or board games where pieces snap to grids (e.g., chess). |
| **Locomotion** | `--locomotion` / `--no-locomotion` | User needs to move around a large virtual space (teleport/slide). Browser-first apps get WASD+mouselook via `browserControls: true`. | User is stationary (e.g., sitting at a desk playing chess). |
| **Grabbing** | `--grabbing` / `--no-grabbing` | User needs to pick up, move, or manipulate objects with hands/controllers. | App is gaze/pointer-only or purely observational. |
| **Scene Understanding** | `--scene-understanding` / `--no-scene-understanding` | AR app needs to know about real-world walls, tables, or floors. | AR app only places objects in mid-air or doesn't interact with the physical environment. |
| **Environment Raycast** | `--environment-raycast` / `--no-environment-raycast` | AR app needs to cast rays against the real-world environment mesh. | AR app doesn't need precise real-world hit testing. |

### Step 4: Choose Source (`--canary`, optional)
- **Default (no flag)**: Uses the latest published npm packages.
- **`--canary`**: Uses a baked-in CloudFront CDN URL for canary SDK bundle (replaces old `--from`).
- **`--canary <url>`**: Custom bundle URL for internal builds.

### Example Scaffolding Commands

```bash
# VR chess game (stationary, no physics needed)
npx @iwsdk/create@0.4.0 chess-app --yes --mode vr --no-physics --no-locomotion --no-metaspatial
[347 more lines in file. Cloud-Friendly Headless Browser (Automatic SwiftShader)\n61:**The manual SwiftShader patching from v0.3.x is no longer needed.**\n65:- Automatically falls back to SwiftShader (Playwright Chromium's bundled software renderer) on GPU-less cloud VMs\n72:IWSDK_GPU=swiftshader   # Force SwiftShader (software rendering)\n75:In the Manus sandbox (no GPU), SwiftShader is selected automatically — just run `npm run dev` or `npx iwsdk dev up`.\n181:# Start dev server (SwiftShader auto-detected in headless environments)\n188:npx iwsdk dev down          # Stop dev server\n195:The dev server launches the headless browser, connects the MCP runtime, and exposes the app on `https://localhost:8081/`.\n239:npx iwsdk reference file --input-json '{\"file_path\":\"src/ecs/world.ts\",\"source\":\"iwsdk\"}'\n260:When the dev server is running, you have access to runtime debugging commands via MCP.\n272:iwsdk ecs <action>\n283:npx iwsdk ecs find --input-json '{\"components\":[\"DistanceGrabbable\"]}'\n284:npx iwsdk ecs query --input-json '{\"entityIndex\":3}'\n285:npx iwsdk ecs set-component --input-json '{\"entityIndex\":3,\"componentId\":\"Transform\",\"field\":\"position\",\"value\":[2,1,-1.8]}'\n290:npx iwsdk ecs pause\n291:npx iwsdk ecs step --input-json '{\"frames\":1}'\n292:npx iwsdk ecs resume\n293:npx iwsdk ecs snapshot --input-json '{\"label\":\"snap1\"}'\n294:npx iwsdk ecs diff --input-json '{\"from\":\"snap1\",\"to\":\"snap2\"}'\n338:If XR commands start timing out or the dev server terminal floods with `\"Unknown device: undefined\"` errors — you almost certainly passed a wrong parameter name to a state-mutating XR command.\n342:2. If that also times out, kill and restart the dev server\n350:npx iwsdk browser screenshot    # Take a screenshot (returns file path)\n395:- **Manual SwiftShader patching** — auto-detected and applied by vite-plugin-dev\n","stderr":"","pty":false,"timedOut":false}","success":true,"type":"function_call_output"},{"arguments":"{"limit":90,"offset":175,"path":"workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md"}","call_id":"toolu_016vkBQfYR2X9aiUNSMRMkEs","name":"read","type":"function_call"},{"call_id":"toolu_016vkBQfYR2X9aiUNSMRMkEs","output":"### Grabbed Component
New `Grabbed` component for physics kinematic override during grab interactions.

## 5. Starting the Dev Server

```bash
# Start dev server (SwiftShader auto-detected in headless environments)
npm run dev

# Or using the CLI directly
npx iwsdk dev up --foreground

# Other dev commands
npx iwsdk dev down          # Stop dev server
npx iwsdk dev restart       # Restart
npx iwsdk dev logs          # View logs
npx iwsdk dev open          # Open in browser
npx iwsdk dev status        # Check status
```

The dev server launches the headless browser, connects the MCP runtime, and exposes the app on `https://localhost:8081/`.

## 6. Adapter Management (NEW in 0.4.0)

MCP adapter configs are now written at project creation instead of at runtime.

```bash
# Sync adapter configs for all detected AI tools
npx iwsdk adapter sync

# Check adapter status
npx iwsdk adapter status

# Remove stale adapter configs
npx iwsdk adapter prune
```

Supported adapters: Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Copilot.

## 7. Using the Reference System

IWSDK 0.4.0 introduces `@iwsdk/reference` — a local RAG reference system with semantic code search.

### Setup
```bash
# Warmup: downloads corpus and embedding model (~120MB, one-time)
npx iwsdk reference warmup

# Check status
npx iwsdk reference status
```

### Query Tools (8 total)
```bash
# Semantic code search
npx iwsdk reference search --input-json '{"query":"how to create a grabbable object","limit":5}'

# API reference lookup
npx iwsdk reference api --input-json '{"name":"World.create"}'

# Find code by relationship
npx iwsdk reference relationship --input-json '{"type":"extends","target":"System"}'

# File content
npx iwsdk reference file --input-json '{"file_path":"src/ecs/world.ts","source":"iwsdk"}'

# List ECS components
npx iwsdk reference components --input-json '{}'

# List ECS systems
npx iwsdk reference systems --input-json '{}'

# Find dependents of an API
npx iwsdk reference dependents --input-json '{"api_name":"DistanceGrabbable"}'

# Find usage examples
npx iwsdk reference examples --input-json '{"api_name":"DistanceGrabbable"}'

# Inspect tool catalog
npx iwsdk reference inspect
npx iwsdk reference inspect --tool search
```

## 8. Runtime Debugging & ECS CLI

When the dev server is running, you have access to runtime debugging commands via MCP.

### Full CLI Command Tree
```

### Scene Inspection
```bash
npx iwsdk scene hierarchy             # Full Three.js scene tree
npx iwsdk scene transform --input-json '{"uuid":"<uuid>"}'  # Local/global transforms
```

### ECS Inspection
```bash
npx iwsdk ecs find --input-json '{"components":["DistanceGrabbable"]}'
npx iwsdk ecs query --input-json '{"entityIndex":3}'
npx iwsdk ecs set-component --input-json '{"entityIndex":3,"componentId":"Transform","field":"position","value":[2,1,-1.8]}'
```

### ECS Time Travel & Snapshots
```bash
npx iwsdk ecs pause
npx iwsdk ecs step --input-json '{"frames":1}'
npx iwsdk ecs resume
npx iwsdk ecs snapshot --input-json '{"label":"snap1"}'
npx iwsdk ecs diff --input-json '{"from":"snap1","to":"snap2"}'
```

### XR Emulation

#### Device Names (CRITICAL)
| Device Name | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `"headset"` | The HMD / viewer |
| `"controller-right"` | Right controller |
| `"controller-left"` | Left controller |
| `"hand-right"` | Right hand |
| `"hand-left"` | Left hand |

Do NOT use any other variants (`"right"`, `"right-controller"`, `"rightController"`, etc.).

#### Common XR Commands
```bash
npx iwsdk xr enter
npx iwsdk xr exit
npx iwsdk xr status
npx iwsdk xr set-transform --input-json '{"device":"headset","position":{"x":0,"y":1.6,"z":-2}}'
npx iwsdk xr look-at --input-json '{"device":"headset","target":{"x":0,"y":0.9,"z":0}}'
npx iwsdk xr animate-to --input-json '{"device":"headset","position":{"x":0,"y":1.5,"z":0},"duration":0.5}'
npx iwsdk xr select --input-json '{"device":"controller-right"}'
npx iwsdk xr get-select-value --input-json '{"device":"controller-right"}'
npx iwsdk xr set-select-value --input-json '{"device":"controller-right","value":1}'
npx iwsdk xr get-gamepad-state --input-json '{"device":"controller-right"}'
npx iwsdk xr set-gamepad-state --input-json '{"device":"controller-right","buttons":[{"index":0,"value":1}]}'
```

#### `set-device-state` Uses a Different Naming Convention
```bash
npx iwsdk xr set-device-state --input-json '{
  "controllers": {
    "right": {
      "position": {"x":0.2, "y":1.1, "z":0.3},
      "orientation": {"x":0, "y":0, "z":0, "w":1}
    }
  }
}'
```

#### If the Runtime Becomes Unresponsive
If XR commands start timing out or the dev server terminal floods with `"Unknown device: undefined"` errors — you almost certainly passed a wrong parameter name to a state-mutating XR command.

**How to recover:**
1. Reload the browser page: `npx iwsdk browser reload`
2. If that also times out, kill and restart the dev server
3. Re-enter XR mode: `npx iwsdk xr enter`
4. Retry the command with the **correct `"device"` parameter**

**Why this happens:** State-mutating commands (`select`, `set-select-value`, `set-gamepad-state`) are enqueued for frame-synchronized execution. When `device` is `undefined`, the queued action fails on every frame in a code path with no error handling.

### Browser Tools
```bash
npx iwsdk browser screenshot    # Take a screenshot (returns file path)
npx iwsdk browser logs           # View console logs
npx iwsdk browser reload         # Hard reload the page
```

## 9. Bundled Claude Code Skills

The starter template includes 7 Claude Code skills in `packages/starter-assets/claude-injections/skills/`:

| Skill | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `iwsdk-planner` | Project planning and architecture |
| `iwsdk-grab` | Grab interaction implementation |
| `iwsdk-ray` | Ray interaction implementation |
| `iwsdk-ui` | Spatial UI implementation |
| `iwsdk-debug` | Debugging workflows |
| `iwsdk-physics` | Physics system implementation |
| `iwsdk-depth-occlusion` | **NEW in 0.4.1.** Depth sensing and occlusion for AR |

## 10. Production Build & Delivery (REQUIRED)

When the application is complete and working, you **MUST always run a production build and deliver the dist folder** to the user.

```bash
npm run build
cd dist && zip -r /home/ubuntu/<project-name>-dist.zip .
```

Deliver the zip file as an attachment. The dist folder is a self-contained static site.

## Migration Notes: 0.3.x → 0.4.x

### Breaking Changes
1. **`world.input` is now `InputManager`**, not `XRInputManager`. Access XR input via `world.input.xr`.
2. **`AssetManager.getGLTF()` returns clones by default.** Pass `{ shared: true }` to get the old shared-instance behavior.
3. **`--from <url>` is now `--canary [url]`** in create CLI.
4. **`ai.tools` removed from vite plugin config.** MCP adapter configs are now project-level, managed via `iwsdk adapter sync`.

### Deprecated (still working)
- `world.input.gamepads` → use `world.input.xr.gamepads`
- `world.input.multiPointers` → use `world.input.xr.multiPointers`
- `world.input.visualAdapters` → use `world.input.xr.visualAdapters`
- `world.input.isPrimary()` → use `world.input.xr.isPrimary()`

### No Longer Needed

item.thinking:
Now I can see the problem. The IWSDK dev server itself runs `npx playwright install` to install chromium, and that install cleans up the 1208 copy we made (because it's "unused" from playwright's perspective). Then it expects 1208 but just deleted it.

The issue is that `npx iwsdk dev up` uses playwright ^1.58.2, which requires revision 1208, but when it runs `npx playwright install`, the top-level playwright (1.59.1, revision 1217) removes 1208 as unused.
 
So the vite plugin declares ^1.58.2 as a dependency, but the installed version is 1.59.1 which satisfies that range. When the plugin runs its own `npx playwright install`, it's installing browsers for 1.59.1 (revision 1217), but something in the process is still expecting 1208.

Looking at the logs, the sequence is clear: the plugin cleans up the old 1208 revision, installs 1217 successfully, but then tries to launch from the 1208 path anyway. This suggests the IWSDK vite plugin has a hardcoded or cached reference to the older revision that doesn't match what it's actually installing.

The version mismatch is puzzling though—I've confirmed that playwright-core 1.59.1 should map to revision 1217, not 1208. Let me reconsider whether the `npx iwsdk dev up` command might be using a different Node.js environment or a different version of playwright than what I'm seeing in the current node_modules. I'm going in circles with the dependency issue—let me step back and focus on what actually matters: Felix says the blocks are spawning backwards, so I need to fix the code itself. item.arguments:
{"command":"# Now let me trace through the math:
python3 << 'EOF'
# Constants
LANE_LENGTH = 20
HIT_ZONE_Z = -2  # where blocks should be hit
SPAWN_Z = HIT_ZONE_Z - LANE_LENGTH  # = -22

APPROACH_TIME = 2.0  # seconds (typical)
speed = LANE_LENGTH / APPROACH_TIME  # = 10 units/sec

print("=== Constants ===")
print(f"HIT_ZONE_Z = {HIT_ZONE_Z}")
print(f"SPAWN_Z = {SPAWN_Z}")  
print(f"speed = {speed}")
print()

# Player is at z=0. Blocks should spawn far away (negative z) 
# and move TOWARD the player (positive z direction)
print("=== In Three.js, -Z is forward (into screen) ===")
print("Player at z=0, HIT_ZONE_Z at z=-2 (just in front of player)")
print(f"SPAWN_Z at z={SPAWN_Z} (far away from player)")
print()

# Current formula: z = HIT_ZONE_Z - timeUntilHit * speed
# Let's trace:
print("=== Tracing block position with CURRENT formula ===")
print("z = HIT_ZONE_Z - timeUntilHit * speed")
print()

for targetTime in [5.0]:
    for songTime in [3.0, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5]:
        timeUntilHit = targetTime - songTime
        z = HIT_ZONE_Z - timeUntilHit * speed
        print(f"  songTime={songTime:.1f}, targetTime={targetTime:.1f}, "
              f"timeUntilHit={timeUntilHit:.1f}, z={z:.1f}")

print()
print("=== Analysis ===")
# At targetTime (when player should hit): timeUntilHit = 0
z_at_hit = HIT_ZONE_Z - 0 * speed
print(f"At hit time: z = {z_at_hit} (should be HIT_ZONE_Z = {HIT_ZONE_Z}) ✓")

# 2 seconds before hit: timeUntilHit = 2.0
z_at_spawn = HIT_ZONE_Z - 2.0 * speed
print(f"At spawn (2s before): z = {z_at_spawn} (should be SPAWN_Z = {SPAWN_Z}) ✓")

# Direction of travel
print()
print(f"Block starts at z={z_at_spawn} and moves to z={z_at_hit}")
print(f"That's from z=-22 to z=-2, which is TOWARD the player at z=0")
print("Direction: -22 → -2 = moving in +Z direction = TOWARD PLAYER ✓")
print()
print("=== After the hit time (block passes player) ===")
z_after = HIT_ZONE_Z - (-0.5) * speed  # timeUntilHit = -0.5
print(f"0.5s after target: z = {z_after} (continues past player)")

# What about the ORIGINAL formula before the "fix"?
print()
print("=== What was the ORIGINAL formula (before subagent fix)? ===")
print("We need to see git diff to know what was changed...")
EOF"}

item.thinking:
OK so the original formula was:
```
z = HIT_ZONE_Z + timeUntilHit * block.speed
```

And the "fix" changed it to:
```
z = HIT_ZONE_Z - timeUntilHit * block.speed
```

Let me trace through both:

**Original: `z = HIT_ZONE_Z + timeUntilHit * speed`**
- timeUntilHit = 2.0 (2 seconds before hit): z = -2 + 2.0 * 10 = -2 + 20 = 18


- timeUntilHit = 1.0: z = -2 + 10 = 8
- timeUntilHit = 0 (at hit): z = -2 + 0 = -2 ✓ (arrives at HIT_ZONE_Z)

So the block starts at z=18 and moves toward z=-2. In Three.js, negative Z is forward (toward the camera), so the block begins behind the player and travels forward to the hit zone in front of them.

With the corrected formula `z = HIT_ZONE_Z - timeUntilHit * speed`, blocks now spawn far ahead at z=-22 and approach the player, which is the right direction for a rhythm game where notes come toward you.

But Felix says it's still backwards, so let me reconsider by looking at the environment setup—the floor and ceiling grids are positioned at z=-10, which suggests the playfield extends in the negative Z direction, confirming blocks should indeed travel from negative Z toward the player. Felix is saying the blocks are still moving backwards even when he actually tests it, so the issue isn't just the math—something about the camera orientation, hit zone markers, or block mesh geometry must be inverted. I need to verify the camera's position and direction, check if the visual hit zone matches the actual detection zone, and examine whether the block mesh itself has any directional indicators that might be misleading.
